semantic_journey: Arabic hadan = calm, quiet (hadana = to calm). Greek hadinon = thick, deep, plentiful (an Homeric hapax meaning dense/crowded). Calmness and thickness/density/plenty are in different domains. Calm encodes stillness; thick/deep/plentiful encodes density. No valid 2-step path from stillness to density.
key_insight: Arabic calmness/quiet and Greek thick/deep/plentiful are in different domains; stillness and density are different concepts.

semantic_journey: Arabic takhara = a place/ethnonym related to the Tocharians. Greek Tokharos = Tocharoi, a Central Asian people (proper name). Both are proper names for the same Central Asian people/region. This is the same proper name transmitted through different language channels. Proper name exclusion applies.
key_insight: Both Arabic takhara and Greek Tokharos are proper names for the same Tocharian Central Asian people; proper name exclusion applies.

semantic_journey: Arabic kabata = to fill with grief/suppress, to be weighed down by sorrow. Greek ek batheon = from the depths (of heart) (ek=from + bathos=depth). Core: deep inner distress. Arabic being-filled-with-grief/suppressed and Greek from-the-depths-of-heart both encode profound inner suffering. 1-step direct preservation: filled-with-suppressed-grief -> from-the-depths (of inner anguish).
key_insight: Arabic suppressed inner grief and Greek from-the-depths-of-heart share the concept of profound inner emotional depth/distress.

semantic_journey: Arabic kabata = suppression/being filled with grief (same root as GRC_0826). Greek ek batheon = from the depths of the heart. Same analysis as GRC_0826. Core: deep inner emotional depth. 1-step: suppressed-grief -> from-the-depths-of-heart.
key_insight: Same root and analysis as GRC_0826; Arabic suppressed grief and Greek from-the-depths share profound inner emotional depth.

semantic_journey: Arabic basala = brave/fierce/courageous warrior (basil = bold warrior). Greek Basileios = royal, kingly (from basileus = king). Arabic brave-warrior and Greek royal/kingly are both leadership/excellence concepts but in different domains: warrior-courage vs royal-rank. The chain warrior -> leader -> king requires 3 steps. Also Greek basileus has its own documented etymology (possibly from earlier Aegean substrate).
key_insight: Arabic brave-warrior and Greek royal/kingly are both leadership concepts but in different domains; warrior-to-king requires 3 steps.

semantic_journey: Arabic lataha = woman giving birth (a description of the birthing process). Greek Eileithuia = Eileithyia, the goddess of childbirth (proper name, well-attested Greek goddess). Eileithyia is a proper Greek divine name with its own mythology. The connection between an Arabic birth-description and a Greek divine name is merely thematic. Proper name exclusion applies.
key_insight: Eileithyia is a proper Greek divine name; Arabic birth-description and a Greek divine name share only the childbirth theme.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-barkhash = uproar, confusion, clamor. Greek brakhesi = dative plural of brakhys = short, brief. The original correctly notes semantic mismatch: clamor and short/brief are in entirely different domains. No valid path exists. Phonological match br-kh-sh is notable but semantics fail completely.
key_insight: Arabic clamor/uproar and Greek short/brief are in entirely different semantic domains; phonological match alone is insufficient.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-baland = root of henna plant. Greek Blanda = a city in Basilicata, Italy (proper name). A henna plant root and an Italian city name share no semantic path. Proper name exclusion applies; even if the toponym has a Mediterranean plant-name substrate, this is speculative without documented evidence.
key_insight: Greek Blanda is a proper Italian toponym; Arabic henna-root and an Italian city share no documented connection.
